coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid mediated organophilic carbon dots for drug delivery and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Yang
Lu, Jun
Mao, Quan-Xing
Song, Ru-Sheng
Wang, Xue-Ying
Chen, Xu-Wei
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: By taking advantage of the structural tunability of ionic liquids (ILs), a series of imidazolium ILs were employed as precursors to prepare carbon dots (IL-CDs) and as regulators to control their properties. The simultaneous formation of hydrophilic CDs (IL-HCDs) and organophilic CDs (IL-OCDs) is achieved in hydrothermal reaction system by undergoing sulfuric acid carbonization. The quantum yields (QY) of IL-OCDs are closely correlated with both the cationic and anionic moieties of the ionic liquids, i.e., longer side chains of cations in the imidazolium ILs and weakly nucleophilic anions tend to produce highly fluorescent IL-OCDs. Both IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, and that of IL-HCDs is even lower than IL-OCDs. A drug delivery system is constructed by combining anticancer drug curcumin (Cur) with IL-OCDs via hydrophobic interaction, among which 1,3-dibutylimidazolium nitrate derived IL-OCDs exhibit highest photoluminescence. In addition, it serves as a favorable drug carrier with high drug loading efficiency and facilitates rapid penetration/transportation of Cur into the cell interior, which significantly accelerates the apoptosis of HeLa cells. This process is further visualized by cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquids
Organophilic carbon dots
Drug delivery
Cell imaging

Experimental
Chemicals and reagents
Instrumentations
Preparation of ionic liquid mediated IL-CDs
Cell incubation and MTT assay
Loading of curcumin on BBIMNO3 oriented OCDs
Results and discussion
Preparation and characterizations of the ionic liquid mediated CDs
The yields of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs
Characteristics of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs
Mechanism for the formation of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs
Ionic liquid orientation on the properties of IL-CDs
ILs on the fluorescent performance of IL-CDs
The cytotoxicity of IL-CDs and cell imaging
Drug delivery with IL-OCDs as carrier
Loading and releasing of anticancer drug curcumin
The cytotoxicity of IL-OCD/Cur drug delivery system
Demonstration of the penetration of IL-OCD/Cur into cells
Conclusions
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate financial supports from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21235010, 21475017, 21575020), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N130105002, N141008001, N140505003, 150502001, N152004005).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images and the size distributions (insets) of BBIMNO3-HCDs (A) and BBIMNO3-OCDs (B); UV–vis absorption spectra of BBIMNO3-HCDs (C-left) and BBIMNO3-OCDs (D-left) (10 μg mL−1 in ethanol); fluorescence emission spectra of BBIMNO3-HCDs (C-right) and BBIMNO3-OCDs (D-right), with the insets as the normalized emission spectra. (4 mg mL−1 in ethanol). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of BBIMNO3-HCDs (A), BBIMNO3-OCDs (B). High resolution spectra of C1s (i), N1s (ii), S2p (iii). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The yields of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs with the variation of reaction time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with 20 μg mL−1 of IL-CDs for 3 h. Taken at bright field (i); with excitation at 340 nm (ii), 495 nm (iii), 550 nm (iv). Scale bar: 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence intensity of BBIMNO3-OCDs (20 μg mL−1) at λ ex/λ em = 280/350 nm in the presence of Cur at various concentrations. (B) Viabilities of HeLa cells after incubation with 20 μg mL−1 BBIMNO3-OCDs, 20 μg mL−1 Cur and 29 μg mL−1 OCDs/Cur conjugate. The untreated HeLa cells were used as control.

The photographs of HeLa cells after incubation for 120 min with 20 μg mL−1 of BBIMNO3-OCDs (A) and 20 μg mL−1 of BBIMNO3-OCDs/Cur (B). Taken at bright field (i); with excitation at 340 nm (ii), 495 nm (iii), 550 nm (iv), merge (v), Scale bar: 20 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration for the preparation of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs.
The structure and water solubility of the 11 ILs and the corresponding yields of IL-CDs (HCDs and OCDs) with these ILs as precursors.
Quantum yields (QYs) of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs with various precursor ionic liquids.

row.entry: ILs with a same anionic counterpart

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid mediated organophilic carbon dots for drug delivery and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110189, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences and Health, Northeastern University, Box H006, Shenyang 110169, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences and Health

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By taking advantage of the structural tunability of ionic liquids (ILs), a series of imidazolium ILs were employed as precursors to prepare carbon dots (IL-CDs) and as regulators to control their properties. The simultaneous formation of hydrophilic CDs (IL-HCDs) and organophilic CDs (IL-OCDs) is achieved in hydrothermal reaction system by undergoing sulfuric acid carbonization. The quantum yields (QY) of IL-OCDs are closely correlated with both the cationic and anionic moieties of the ionic liquids, i.e., longer side chains of cations in the imidazolium ILs and weakly nucleophilic anions tend to produce highly fluorescent IL-OCDs. Both IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, and that of IL-HCDs is even lower than IL-OCDs. A drug delivery system is constructed by combining anticancer drug curcumin (Cur) with IL-OCDs via hydrophobic interaction, among which 1,3-dibutylimidazolium nitrate derived IL-OCDs exhibit highest photoluminescence. In addition, it serves as a favorable drug carrier with high drug loading efficiency and facilitates rapid penetration/transportation of Cur into the cell interior, which significantly accelerates the apoptosis of HeLa cells. This process is further visualized by cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (carbon dots, CDs) have shown promising potentials in bio-imaging and drug delivery due to their low toxicity, favorable photostability and ease of surface functionalization [1–3]. Most CDs reported so far are hydrophilic in nature, while organophilic/hydrophobic CDs (OCDs) with good solubility are highly desired for studies in hydrophobic environment, e.g., cell membrane. In terms of drug delivery, many anticancer drugs are hydrophobic, OCDs is an exclusive and suitable alternative as nanomedicine carrier to improve the drug loading rate [4–6], and facilitate fast penetration of cell membrane which ensure high drug delivery efficiency However, studies on OCDs is far less than that for hydrophilic CDs (HCDs). A few approaches were reported for the preparation of OCDs, where hydrophobic long-chain organic molecules, e.g., dodecanethiol, hexadecylamine and poloxamer, are predominantly used as surface passivation/capping agents [7–9]. Another strategy is the modification of HCDs surface with hydrophobic moietyto tune/regulate the hydrophobicity of the CDs [10].

As is known that ionic liquids (ILs) consist of organic cations and organic/inorganic anions [11–13]. The intrinsic non-molecular natures of ILs give rise to unique physico-chemical properties. Especially their cations and anions are diverse and changeable, which makes it feasible to precisely adjust/regulate the characters of ILs. By taking advantage of this merit, ILs have been used as carbon sources for the preparation of ionic liquid mediated CDs, by microwave-assisted reaction, hydrothermal treatment as well as sulfuric acid carbonization [14–18]. The hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the obtained CDs can be readily tuned/regulated by anion exchange of the ionic liquid moiety grafting on CDs surface [10]. In our previous work, a one-pot procedure was developed for the preparation of both IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate as the carbon source in a H3PO4-ethanol medium [19]. The mass proportion of HCDs to OCDs is simply regulated by varying the H3PO4/ethanol molar ratio. It is further found that the modification of CDs with ILs significantly improved their biocompatibility [20], and IL-OCDs penetrate the cell membrane via multiple pathways, which facilitate fast penetration of IL-OCDs into HeLa cell interior within 1 min [21].

In biological studies, especially bioimagings, the fluorescent quantum yield of CDs is highly important to achieve bright and clear images for biological cells. The above studies indicated that ionic liquids have been applied for regulating the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of CDs, while so far there is no report on the demonstration of regulating the quantum yield of CDs with ionic liquids. In the present study, 11 imidazolium ILs were used as precursors for the preparation of IL-CDs, meanwhile, ILs serve as regulators to control the properties of the obtained CDs. The length of side chain of the imidazolium cation and the structure of anions in the ionic liquids pose significant influence on the fluorescence behavior, and cell imaging capacity of the obtained CDs. Furthermore, organophilic fluorescent CDs have been demonstrated to be a suitable drug delivery carrier and exhibit favorable biocompability with high drug loading efficiency, which ensures rapid penetration of the CDs into the cell interior and facilitates cell imaging therein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Chemicals and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The following ionic liquids are purchased from Shanghai Cheng Jie Chemicals Co. LTD (Shanghai, China), i.e., 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (EMIMBF4), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BMIMBF4), 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (HMIMBF4), 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (OMIMBF4), 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (DMIMBF4), 1,3-dibutylimidazolium chlorine (BBIMCl), 1,3-dibutylimidazolium bromine (BBIMBr), 1,3-dibutylimidazolium nitrate (BBIMNO3), 1,3-dibutylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BBIMBF4), 1,3-dibutylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BBIMPF6) 1,3-dibutylimidazolium bis((trifluoromethyl)sulfonyl)imide (BBIMTf2N). Curcumin (Cur), sulfuric acid is obtained from Sino-pharm Chemical Reagent (Shenyang, China) and used as received. Dulbecco's modified medium (DMEM, high glucose), trypsin, penicillin/streptomycin and fetal bovine serum are obtained from Thermo Scientific (Logan, Utah, USA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit is the product of Nanjing KeyGEN Biotech (Nanjing, China). Other chemicals employed are at least of analytical reagent grade. Deionized water of 18 MΩ cm−1 is used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumentations
ce:section.ce:para: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the CDs were recorded on a JEM-2100 F microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. The specimen was prepared by drop-casting the sample aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL) onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet-6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) by using KBr pellets. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a JASCO V-550 UV/visible spectrophotometer (JASCO International Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were performed on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) providing a 0.5 cm quartz cuvette. The quantum yields of the CDs were recorded on a Quantaurus-QY absolute photoluminescence quantum yield measurement system (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a MPDDY2094 X-ray diffractometer (PAnalytical B.V., Netherlands) with Cu-Kα irradiation (λ 1.5406 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were conducted with an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc, USA). Cell imaging studies were performed on a fluorescence micro-imaging system (UltraVIEW VoX, Perkin Elmer, America).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ionic liquid mediated IL-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ionic liquid mediated carbon dots (IL-CDs) were prepared by sulfuric acid carbonization of the corresponding ionic liquids. 1 g of a specific ionic liquid was added into 4 mL of H2SO4-ethanol solution (12%, m/m) in a 25-mL hydrothermal reactor. The mixture is transferred into an electric thermostatic drier and kept at 200 °C for 48 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant dark brown solution was neutralized with fresh NaOH-ethanol solution. The sediment was removed by 10000 rpm centrifugation for 10 min. The supernatant was dialyzed by molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100–500 against ultrapure water for 72 h. The hydrophilic CDs (HCDs) dissolved in water and were obtained by freeze drying of the aqueous medium, while the organophilic CDs (OCDs) gathered in the inner surface of the dialysis bag and were collected by decanting the HCDs aqueous solution. Finally the OCDs were dialyzed against ethanol to remove the impurity and dried under vacuum. HCDs and OCDs were dissolved in ethanol for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell incubation and MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics penicillin and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2.

HeLa cells within replicate 96-well plates were pre-cultured at standard culture conditions for 24 h. After removal of the medium, cells were incubated with fresh media containing IL-CDs (100 μg mL−1 for IL-OCDs, 200 μg mL−1 for IL-HCDs) for extra 20 h. 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was then added to each well, and after incubated for 4 h, the MTT solution was removed. 100 μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the formed crystals. The absorbance was measured at 490 nm using a Polarstar microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of curcumin on BBIMNO3 oriented OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the loading of anticancer drug Cur, 80 μg mL−1 Cur ethanol solution was mixed with 20 μg mL−1 BBIMNO3 oriented organophilic OCDs in ethanol. The resulting Cur/OCDs nanocomposite was collected by dialysis against 50% ethanol aqueous solution to remove the free or unadsorbed Cur (Cur in the solution not retained by the OCDs). The outside dialysis solution was collected and the concentration of Cur is determined by measuring the absorbance at 425 nm to evaluate the loading ratio of Cur, and the mass of unadsorbed Cur (muCur) is derived. The loading ratio of Cur onto OCDs is expressed as: (mCur−muCur)/mCDs × 100%, with mCur and mCDs as the mass of original Cur and OCDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Preparation and characterizations of the ionic liquid mediated CDs
The yields of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 11 imidazolium ILs were chosen as precursors to prepare the IL-CDs for the purpose of evaluating their effect on the properties of IL-CDs. The ILs used in this study were divided into 2 categories. The first group included 5 ILs with the same anion [BF4 −] and different side chain of cations (EMIMBF4, BMIMBF4, HMIMBF4, OMIMBF4, DMIMBF4). The second group contained 6 ILs with the same cation [BBIM+] and various anions (BBIMCl, BBIMBr, BBIMBF4, BBIMPF6, BBIMTf2N). The structures and water solubilities of these ILs are listed in Table 1 . 1.0 g of each imidazolium IL was subjected to the hydrothermal treatment as described in the experimental section. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs were simultaneously obtained in the present preparation strategy. The yields of IL-HCDs varied from 2.69 to 8.35%, while those for IL-OCDs were found in the range of 0–24.8%. For the first group of ILs, the yields of IL-OCDs increased with the length of the side chain of cations in the corresponding precursor ILs, while for the second group, the yields of IL-OCDs increased with the size of the corresponding anionic counterparts.

The OCDs exhibit high dispersity in a wide variety of organic solvents including dimethyl sulfoxide, methanol, ethanol, acetone, iso-propyl alcohol, dichloromethane and isobutanol, while it is slightly dispersed in methylbenzene. The above dispersions of OCDs were stable for 6 months without any precipitation. In addition, HCDs and OCDs exhibit strong greenish-blue photoluminescence. The digital images of BBIMNO3 oriented OCDs dispersed in various organic solvents were shown in the supporting information Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs were subjected to the comparative characterizations. HR-TEM images of particle-size distributions and morphologies of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs are given (Fig. 1 A and B). The average particle size of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs are 5.8 and 7.2 nm, respectively. The relatively smaller particle size of HCDs might be attributed to the large electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance among the surface groups on CDs. That is to say more functional groups present on the surface of BBIMNO3-HCDs restrict their growth. In addition, XRD patterns of HCDs and OCDs (Fig. S2) exhibit a wide peak at around 2θ = 23° which clearly indicated the amorphous nature of the material.

UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1, C-D) illustrated that both BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs exhibit an absorption at around 230 nm, which is assigned to the π-π* transitions of CC [22,23]. The stronger absorbance of BBIMNO3-OCDs with respect to that of BBIMNO3-HCDs implied that BBIMNO3-OCDs possess more conjugated aromatic structure.

The excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 1, C-D) indicated an emission maximum at ca. 420 nm for BBIMNO3-HCDs with excitation at 290–330 nm, and the fluorescence emission increases with the increase of excitation wavelength up to 350 nm. Thereafter, when exciting at even longer wavelengths, red-shifts of the fluorescence maximum are observed with progressive decline of the emission intensity. The excitation-independent blue emission is molecular-like fluorescence and comes from CO bonds of CDs, while the excitation-dependent red-shift emission is attributed to “defect states” with a broad energy distribution on CDs [24]. For the case of BBIMNO3-OCDs, red-shift of the fluorescence maximum from 360 to 560 nm is observed with excitation wavelengths from 290 to 470 nm. The absence of excitation-independent emission behavior indicated the decrement of the CO bonds in BBIMNO3-OCDs with respect to that in BBIMNO3-HCDs. In addition, it is known that the size of CDs plays a role in their fluorescent emission [25]. Thus the bigger size of BBIMNO3-OCDs could be an extra contribution to the excitation-dependent emission behavior.

It is worth mentioning that most imidazolium ionic liquids exhibit similar excitation-dependent emission, attributed to the existence of energetically different associated species. With variation of excitation wavelength, a slightly different associated species is excited and an emission characteristic of this particular species was observed [26]. The “defect” structure of CDs, as well as the IL groups on CDs surface synergistically leads to the excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior of IL-CDs. The λ ex/λ em of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs are 350/430 nm, 370/460 nm, respectively. The red shift of OCD is probably ascribed to the improvement of conjugations of π-π* bond related to BBIMNO3-HCDs [27]. In addition, OCDs have larger particle size in comparison with HCDs, which results in a decrease of the HOMO-LUMO gap on the fragment of CDs, and leads to red-shift emission of OCDs with respect to HCDs [25,28].

Both BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs exhibit concentration-dependent photoluminescence behavior. For BBIMNO3-HCDs, the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths shift from 290/390 nm to 350/430 nm with the concentration increase from 0.5 mg mL−1 to 5.8 mg mL−1. The aggregation of IL-CDs at higher concentration results in a decrease of the energy gap and causes red-shift of the excitation/emission wavelength [21]. 3D-fluorescence spectra of BBIMNO3-HCDs with different concentrations are given in Fig. S3.

In FT-IR spectra of BBIMNO3, BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs (Fig. S4), typical absorptions of imidazole ring, i.e., vibration of the imidazole backbone at 1568 cm−1, vibration of alkyl side chain of the imidazole ring at 2855 cm−1 and 2960 cm−1 were identified [29,30]. This clearly demonstrated the presence of the precursor ionic liquid BBIMNO3 on the surface of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs. The absorption at 1115 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO bond, as identified in both spectra of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs. This well illustrated the presence of CO group on the surface of IL-CDs [31]. Meanwhile, the absorption at 1633 cm−1 in the spectrum of BBIMNO3-OCDs belongs to CC stretching vibration of aromatic ring, indicating that BBIMNO3-OCDs contain more conjugated structures, which makes BBIMNO3-OCDs more organophilic.

Raman spectra of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs (Fig. S4) identified an absorption at 1580 cm−1 (G-band) related to the vibration of sp2-bound carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. D-band at ca. 1360 cm−1 is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the plane terminations of disordered graphite or glassy carbons [32]. D/G intensity ratio (I(D)/I(G)) is an indicator of the graphitic ordering [33]. In the present case, I(D)/I(G) ratio of 3.591 for BBIMNO3-HCDs and 0.624 for BBIMNO3-OCDs clearly indicated that BBIMNO3-OCDs are more ordered than BBIMNO3-HCDs. The absorption band at 1475 cm−1 as illustrated in Fig. S4B is attributed to the vibration of alkyl chain in the ionic liquid [34]. The same absorption band is also observed in the Raman spectrum of pure ionic liquid BBIMNO3 (Fig. S4D).

The chemical compositions and structures of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs were further investigated by XPS (Fig. 2 ). Both BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs shows four strong peaks at around 163.7, 285.0, 399.7, and 531.8 eV, which correspond to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s. The contents of C, O, N, S in BBIMNO3-HCDs are 72.05%, 13.58%, 10.25% and 1.44%, respectively. For BBIMNO3-OCDs, the increase of carbon (83.64%) along with decreases of the other three elements (10.48% for O, 5.16% for N, 0.72% for S) demonstrated that OCDs have undergone further carbonization.

High resolution XPS spectra of C1s for both BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs exhibit two main peaks at 284.5 and 285.5 eV, which could be attributed to sp2 carbon (CC, CC), sp3 carbon (CN, CO, CS), respectively [35]. N1s spectrum for BBIMNO3-HCDs could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 400.5 and 401.2 eV, which should be contributed to the presence of pyrrolic nitrogen and sp3 hybridized nitrogen. For BBIMNO3-OCDs, the peak of pyrrolic nitrogen vanished while those of pyridinic type nitrogen at 399.2 eV and NO type nitrogen at 406.0 eV were identified [36]. These results indicated that BBIMNO3-HCDs might transform to BBIMNO3-OCDs by denitrogenation and/or oxidation. S2p spectrum showed that the peaks of sulfinyl sulfur for BBIMNO3-HCDs at 166.3 eV and 167.4 eV were replaced by that of sulfide sulfur at 163.5 eV for BBIMNO3-OCDs, which indicated desulfuration and oxidation during the process of transforming from BBIMNO3-HCDs to BBIMNO3-OCDs [37]. We previously demonstrated that there are ionic liquid moieties grafting on CDs when using ILs as carbon source [20]. The anionic counterpart of IL could be replaced by metathesis reaction. The S2p peak at 168.3 eV, assigned to sulfate sulfur, was identified for both BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs. The SO4 2− and NO3 − are supposed to be the counter anion of imidazole cation grafting on the surface of IL-CDs.

The above characterizations illustrated that BBIMNO3-OCDs have larger particle-size, higher carbon content, less functional group and more ordered structure with respect to BBIMNO3-HCDs. The transformation of moieties between BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs is illustrated in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the formation of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: During the hydrothermal treatment, the chemical structures of the precursors undergo complicated and unpredictable transformations and condensation. The pyrolysis of ILs in the condensed phase was more complicated than the decomposition of one single molecular species [38]. In this case, IL-HCDs were probably formed by fragmentation of IL. The thermally instable domains such as imidazole moieties are transformed into carbon by dehydrogenation and denitrogenation, owing to the oxidative nature of sulfuric acid. It is supposed that the acidic methylene group (CH2 ) on the side chain of the imidazolium cation was activated and incorporated into the carbon framework [39]. After the formation of carbon core, partial passivation via condensation with hydroxyl groups resulted in COC bonds [9].

As mentioned earlier that IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs are obtained simultaneously. The yields of BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs with reaction time were further investigated. In the previous stage of the treatment, both BBIMNO3-HCDs and BBIMNO3-OCDs are obtained and their yields were found to increase within 48 h. At a reaction time of 72 h, the yield of BBIMNO3-HCDs decreased dramatically and that for BBIMNO3-OCDs increased, indicating that IL-HCDs undergo further carbonization to form IL-OCDs (Fig. 3 ). The structural difference of BBIMNO3-OCDs, e.g., larger particle-size, high carbon content and less functional group, with respect to BBIMNO3-HCDs further proved the above speculation. Therefore, the preparation of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs were supposed to include two steps, i.e., the formation of IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs, followed by further carbonation of IL-HCDs to transform into IL-OCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionic liquid orientation on the properties of IL-CDs
ILs on the fluorescent performance of IL-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of precursor ILs on the quantum yields (QYs) of corresponding IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs was thoroughly investigated, and the results were given in Table 2 . Although the QYs of IL-HCDs are related to IL, no regular pattern could be summarized. In general, IL-HCDs gave lower QYs with respect to IL-OCDs. It means that more ordered carbon structure and less active moiety facilitate the improvement of QYs for IL-CDs.

As for IL-OCDs, among the first group of ILs with the same anion [BF4 −] and different side chain of cations (ethyl, butyl, hexyl, octyl, decyl), an obvious increase of QYs is observed by increasing the length of side chain of the imidazole cation. It is known that polymers are usually used to passivate the surface of CDs and increase fluorescent emission and QY [40]. In the present case, ILs with longer alkyl chain might act like polymer and tend to modify the “defect” of CDs, which endow the resultant IL-OCDs with highly fluorescent nature.

We previously found that BBIMCl exhibits a QY of 52.3% attributed to its symmetrical plane conjugating structure [41]. For the second group of ILs containing [BBIM+] cation, the QY of IL-OCDs was found to strongly dependent on the anionic counterpart (Cl−, Br−, BF4 −, PF6 −, Tf2N−), in the order of BBIMTf2N-OCDs > BBIMPF6-OCDs > BBIMBF4-OCDs > BBIMBr-OCDs > BBIMNO3-OCDs. No OCDs were obtained with BBIMCl as precursor, probably due to the low thermal stabilities or strong nucleophilicities of [Cl−]. A similar result was obtained by using BBIMCl for preparing mesoporous carbon material, where replacing [Cl−] with [HSO4 −] resulted in a drastic decrease in carbon yield [38]. In the present case, ILs with weakly nucleophilic anions tends to produce highly fluorescent IL-OCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytotoxicity of IL-CDs and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of IL-CDs was studied with HeLa cells. The experimental results indicated that at 50 μg mL−1 of IL-HCDs and 20 μg mL−1 of IL-OCDs, the cell viability of >80% was maintained (Fig.S5). This observation indicated that both CDs are of low cytotoxicity, and the HCDs are even more biocompatible, which is of highly importance in biological imaging applications. The dependence of cytotoxicity of either IL-OCDs or IL-HCDs on the side chain of ionic liquid was further investigated. Fig. S5 illustrated that after incubating with 100 μg mL−1 of BBIMTf2N-OCDs or BBIMNO3-OCDs, cell viability of 44% or 14% are observed, respectively. On the other hand, the cytotoxicities of IL-HCDs are found to follow the sequence of BBIMTf2N-HCDs < BMIMBr-HCDs ≈ BBIMBF4-HCDs ≈ BBIMPF6-HCDs < BBIMNO3-HCDs.

IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs were used for cell imaging at a concentration level of 20 μg mL−1 and with HeLa cells as model. IL-OCDs showed a much higher capacity of cell imaging in comparison with IL-HCDs. The fluorescence intensity from BBIMNO3-OCDs cultured HeLa cell was 4-fold higher than that incubated with BBIMNO3-HCDs with excitation at 495 nm (Fig.S6). Base on Overton's rule [42], hydrophobic carbon dots, i.e., BBIMNO3-OCDs, can readily and rapidly penetrate into the cell interior, which is much easier than that for hydrophilic carbon dots, i.e., BBIMNO3-HCDs in this particular case. The rapid penetration and accumulation of IL-OCD inside the cells lead to much higher cell imaging capacity, although the quantum yield of BBIMNO3-OCDs is less than that of BBIMNO3-HCDs.

For IL-OCDs, the dependence of cell imaging on ILs structure was exploited (Fig. 4 ). IL-OCDs derived from ILs with different cationic and anionic moieties showed cytoplasmic localization. The capacity of cell imaging by IL-OCDs increased with the length of side chain in the cation of IL for EMIMBF4-OCDs, BMIMBF4-OCDs, HMIMBF4-OCDs, OMIMBF4-OCDs. However, it is noticed that HeLa cells incubated with DMIMBF4-OCDs was the darkest in spite of its highest QY value, due to probably the endocytosis of IL-OCDs and their interaction with the organelles. When referring to the effect of anionic moiety of IL on cell imaging behavior by IL-OCDs, it is noticed that BMIMNO3-OCDs exhibited the highest capacity for cell imaging. [NO3 −] based IL have been found to show great influence on the protein structures because [NO3 − ] is prone to enter the protein interior and therein disrupt the essential intra-protein hydrogen bonds [43]. It seems that similar interactions are present between BMIMNO3-CDs and proteins in the cells. The strong interactions between [NO3 −] and proteins might accelerate the accumulation of BMIMNO3-OCDs, and therein give rise to high fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug delivery with IL-OCDs as carrier
Loading and releasing of anticancer drug curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above discussions, the IL-CDs should be promising vehicles for drug delivery due to their biocompability, tunable hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, and favorable fluorescence property. As a model drug, curcumin (Cur) is a polyphenolic pigment extracted from the root of Curcuma. Longa, with chemical structure as shown in Scheme S1. It has strong antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial and anti-carcinogenic activities, which has been widely used in medicinal and pharmaceutical studies [44,45]. The hydrophobic nature of Cur requires an organophilic carrier for its loading and delivery. Considering the organophilic nature of IL-OCDs, their hydrophobic interaction with Cur might be a driving force for Cur loading and delivery. For this purpose, various concentrations of Cur solution are mixed with BBIMNO3-OCDs to testify the formation of OCD/Cur drug delivery system. An obvious decrement on the fluorescence intensity of BBIMNO3-OCDs was observed with the increase of Cur concentration within 0–120 μg mL−1 by excitation at λ ex 280 nm (Fig. 5 A). The inner filter effects, non-radiative recombination pathways and electron transfer may involved in the process and give rise to the quenching of fluorescence emission of IL-OCDs [14,46]. The drug loading ratio, i.e., mass ratio of the loaded Cur to BBIMNO3-OCDs, is derived to be 69.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytotoxicity of IL-OCD/Cur drug delivery system
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of IL-OCD/Cur on tumor cells was further investigated. HeLa cells were separately cultured for 24 h with 20 μg mL−1 of BBIMNO3-OCDs, 20 μg mL−1 of Cur, or 29 μg mL−1 of IL-OCD/Cur conjugate (containing same amount of Cur as in the free form). The viability of HeLa cells was then determined by MTT assay (Fig. 5B). It is obvious that favorable viabilities of HeLa cells of 87.5% and 95.4% were achieved after incubating with 20 μg mL−1 of IL-OCDs and 20 μg mL−1 of Cur, respectively. However, a much lower cell viability of 20.3% was observed when incubating with the IL-OCD/Cur system. This result clearly demonstrated that the IL-OCD/Cur drug delivery system gives rise to a significant improvement on the transportation of Cur into the cell interior, and therein kills the cells due to accumulation of the drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demonstration of the penetration of IL-OCD/Cur into cells
ce:section.ce:para: After incubation with BBIMNO3-OCDs (20 μg mL−1) and BBIMNO3-OCD/Cur, HeLa cells were real-time monitored by confocal microscope (Fig. 6 A). An increase of fluorescent emission inside HeLa cells was observed in 2 h as BBIMNO3-OCDs gradually entered into the cell interior, and the favorable biocompatibility of BBIMNO3-OCDs keeps the cell alive. When incubating with BBIMNO3-OCD/Cur, the cells started to shrink (Fig. 6B). Recently we found that 1-ethyl-3-methyl imidazolium bromide derived OCDs penetrate the cell membrane via multiple pathways, which facilitates fast penetration into HeLa cells within 1 min. In the present case, rapid uptake of BBIMNO3-OCD/Cur by HeLa cells took place, and fluorescence emission from the cells enhanced significantly. On one hand, the accumulation of BBIMNO3-OCDs contributed to the increase of fluorescence. On the other hand, the apoptosis of cells enhanced their intrinsic fluorescence. The images of HeLa cells after incubation with 20 μg mL−1 of BBIMNO3-OCDs/Cur illustrated a favorable killing rate of HeLa cells within 50 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The sulfuric acid carbonization of ionic liquid precursors produces hydrophilic carbon dots (IL-HCDs) and hydrophobic (IL-OCDs) in a hydrothermal system. IL-OCDs possess larger particle size, higher carbon content and more ordered carbons. In addition, the quantum yields of IL-OCDs could be readily improved by increasing the length of side chain of the imidazolium cation, and decreasing the nucleophilicities of anionic counterparts. As a drug carrier and delivery system, IL-OCDs accelerated the transportation of anticancer drug Cur into cell interior and therein effectively killed the cells. The present study provides a new approach for the regulation of fluorescent properties of carbon dots or other nanostructures, for the purpose of performing different biological studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial supports from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21235010, 21475017, 21575020), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N130105002, N141008001, N140505003, 150502001, N152004005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.12.038.
